scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is explicitly critiquing the Spartan regulations for the office of the Elders.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes these Spartan regulations as being in effect 'at present' during his floruit in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text describes a specific Spartan law regarding poll-tax and government participation.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated by Aristotle at his floruit (-348) as a current functional difference between Sparta and Crete.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule describes the communal maintenance and public mess-tables specific to the Cretan system.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes these Cretan arrangements as existing contemporary to his writing in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is detailing the specific voting powers and structure of the Cretan Assembly.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Presented as a contemporary constitutional feature of Cretan society during Aristotle's floruit in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule identifies the clan-based restrictions for the office of Cosmos in Crete.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle documents this as a current practice in Crete during his floruit in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is describing the unique right of assembly speech within the Carthaginian constitution.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle compares this favorably to other contemporary systems during his floruit in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The rule specifies how wealth is used as a qualification for supreme magistrates in Carthage.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes this as the current law ('this law makes wealth...') of Carthage in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is describing the historical property classes established by Solon, which served as a past precedent.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The rule refers to Solon's specific legislative reforms in Athens, traditionally dated to -594.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is discussing the foundational and ongoing democratic practice of jury membership in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Though mentioning Solon as the founder, the practice of jury service by all citizens was the defining feature of Aristotle's contemporary Athens in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle writes from Athens; the requirement for a metic to have a patron (prostates) is a standard Athenian legal norm.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated as a contemporary legal condition for resident aliens during Aristotle's floruit in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle's definitions of citizenship stages reflect the civic norms of the Athenian polis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the contemporary Athenian classification of minors at the time Aristotle wrote in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is describing the status of retired or 'superannuated' citizens in the context of Greek city-state norms, likely Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Describes contemporary civic status and discharge rules during Aristotle's floruit in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is explicitly citing the laws of Androdamas of Rhegium for the Chalcidians.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle cites these laws as part of his contemporary survey of legislation during his floruit in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle's definition of a citizen based on deliberative and judicial office reflects the Athenian model.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: This is Aristotle's primary contemporary definition of citizenship during his floruit in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The requirement of dual descent for citizenship was a famous contemporary law in Athens (Pericles' Law).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle describes this as the 'in practice' current rule in many poleis, including his own, in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is discussing education for leadership within his contemporary political theory framework.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Stated as a contemporary observation by Aristotle in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The master-slave social structure described is the norm of Aristotle's own society.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Aristotle is describing the contemporary domestic and legal reality of the 4th-century BCE Greek household.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is describing the historical development of constitutions leading up to his time.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: While mentioning 'old times', Aristotle frames this within his contemporary analysis of democratic development in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The status of the sons of citizens as 'incomplete citizens' describes the Athenian legal reality.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the contemporary Athenian legal distinction for minors during Aristotle's life in -348.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Aristotle is analyzing different forms of contemporary Greek constitutions, including Aristocracies.
